speaker: interviewer
text: It Is October 4, 1996. So you were mentioning the-- the leaders of the Jewish community were not really able to do much of anything at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Because, you know, they were leaders, you know, whatever they had to do within the community. But nothing to help or do anything for you. You were actually on your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were no longer allowed to go to school. You weren't allowed to walk--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --certain streets, even.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. We walked. But you know, you were always, especially when we-- we wore the-- the star of David, the Jewish star, that we were identified. And we really couldn't go anyplace. And-- and you know, even the food became rationed, too. You know. So you had, like, books, and you cut out. And not everybody wanted to sell you things. It was a terrible time, as everybody knows. And we just stayed there. Because in '41, already started the-- the razzia. They call razzias. They came with wagons. And they took all the people on the wagons, and they put them in-- in a camp, in a gathering camp. As a matter of fact, that school that I mentioned to you before, that I went to in the beginning, became a gathering camp for people to-- they brought them there. And from there, they dispersed them to, mostly, to Poland, in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, you're not in school. You're sort of restricted a little bit in-- in your movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must tell you what we did for a-- for a while. We worked on the Jewish cemetery. Friends of mine and we-- we-- this we got through the Jewish community. There was a Jewish-- there's a Jewish cemetery in Vienna. It's-- it's-- [GERMAN], it's called. And on the-- there is, like, four different kinds of, they call it doors. One is for Christian and-- and one is for-- till the number four is-- was the Jewish cemetery. So-- and also on the two were Jewish. Because they were the prominent Jewish people who were buried there. And just to pass the time and do something, a-- a-- a bunch of us kids worked on the cemetery. We cleaned the graves. We took the leaves off. We-- we took care of the grass. And that was-- kind of our-- took our time. And-- and-- and we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember we ate lunch-- we had lunch in the-- in where the death people-- where-- I mean, there were no death people in there. But that's where they prepared them. That's where we sat. Or we sat on the graves and we ate. It's morbid. But we were children. And this is-- where else could we have sat? But it brought us together. And we laughed. And we-- [LAUGHS] it kind of passed our time. Mm-hmm. You mentioned that deportations began in-- in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what started to happen? How was this announced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was kind of-- people got letters in the mail, that they have to appear with-- with the luggage that they could carry, in that gathering camp, in the [GERMAN]. And from there, they were packed together and were sent away. Now, nobody knew where. And this is how it started. And also, they had, like, I don't know what you say that in English, but they have, like, actions. Like-- they're called razzia in German and it's actions. They came with-- with trucks, pulled people out from their apartments, said, you have one hour to pack up whatever you can, took him on the tracks, brought him over there, without invitation, and they were sent away. And so it went on and on. And-- and it started and-- and constantly. Also, while we lived in this apartment, every week they had another action. If they needed wool, wool clothing, they came in the [? SR, ?] pushed open the doors, knocked, pushed open, went through-- through all the closets, and took whatever they need. Or they collected silver, or gold, forget about it. Everything they needed, they wanted. You were like an open door. Everybody could come in and out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take things from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took constantly. Constantly. And there was a-- a-- story what my brother did. My brother-- we lived together. And he wanted-- he loved to go to the movies. And he loved to go to the-- to the amusement park that I mentioned, that we used to go. But Jews were not allowed to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went with a couple of friends. He went in there. And they caught him. He was, that time-- maybe this was 1940-- so he was around-- he was 13 years old. And they caught him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my parents were terrible, terrible upset. Because that-- they-- they started already with the actions, sending people away. And he got-- he got sentenced to three days in jail. And from there, we were supposed to be sent right with the family, back to-- to the place, [GERMAN], and from there, wherever we-- they send us. And I remember, my mother went to-- and begged-- he worked in a place there. He worked, also, my brother worked someplace. And she went to the master there. And he-- she said, please, please call the jail. And please do that. And tell them that he's very important to you. So they'll-- should let him out direct to work. And he succeeded doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were safe-- [LAUGHS] you know. We had a couple of times that we were saved that we didn't be-- we are not sent away, before we were actually sent away in '42. And, of course, he got it from my father that time that he shouldn't do that. But he was a kid. He wanted to-- he wanted-- you know, he didn't understand it so fully, the consequences of that. You mentioned that your brother was roughly 13 at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he able to have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. He didn't have a bar mitzvah. We had-- after that-- don't forget, there was not even a synagogue around. There was nothing around. It was destroyed. And-- and it was just a very, very tough, tough time, especially for grown-ups, and also for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned, now you had to share this apartment with four other families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did all of these families get along? And did-- were there any other children your age in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --group?

speaker: interviewee
text: --were no children. There were no children. There were just the two doc-- professors. They had-- didn't have any children. There was a Turkish family. They didn't have children. And there was two sisters, you know, spinster sisters, who lived there too. They had another apartment. And us. So we-- we were the only children in that apartment. Well, every woman had, I guess, when they were cooking, they had to make a schedule. Who is where and when. The bathroom was more important. [LAUGHS] That, we had to really line up, and say, I'm next, and I'm next, and-- this is-- this is how it worked. But, you know-- after whatever came after, this was still bearable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. You-- you mentioned there were a few instances where your family was able to avoid deportation, besides--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some of the others.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other time was 1941. My grandfather had already, you know, the notification that he has to appear there with his wife, who was his second wife. She was-- he was, like, maybe, not even 70. And she was 46. She was much younger. And he married her, and you know, they got along very well. She had children in England. But I don't know anything about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandfather had already the invitation, for, let's say, for the next week or two weeks. And there was an action. No. We had a-- a Turkish family in our-- lived with us. And they came to get this family, you know, to-- to-- you know, with a truck, and all these things. And then they said, and all the rest of them have to come, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one professor who was there, the-- the English professor, Dr. Kornitzer, had a non-Jewish wife. So he didn't have to go. He could stay in the apartment. But the rest of them, we all had-- they gave us a-- maybe an hour or two to pack up, and-- and take us, you know, they picked us up and brought us to this camp. And when my grandfather heard that, that we are in there, he said, I'm not going to wait two weeks. I go myself before, so at least I'm together with my family, with my daughter and her children. And so we were all together, in-- in-- in that camp, lying on the floor with our suitcase. I don't know if we had, we had knapsacks. Whatever we had, we were not prepared for that. And my father knew a few people there who worked in that camp, you know, they did all kinds of menial work. Mainly, carrying the suitcases from-- from the cars and whatever they-- they arrested us with, into the camp. And so they gave him these heavy suitcases or whatever it is, and let him prance in front of the SS who was standing there like this and watching the people. And one who was there a longer time already, a guy, said, this is a good man. We could use him. He's a very good worker. He's very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, all right. So let him do these things. They said, but his family is here. He says, let them go home. So we were saved. That was 1941. But unfortunately, my grandfather was in already. And he was sent away to what we found out later, to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, we found out that the whole group was killed, was shot. Not gassed. They shot them into a-- a grove there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at this point your immediate family was allowed to go home, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my brother, and I went back to the apartment where the professor still is. The rest of it was-- they were all sent away, never heard from. But we were just saved another year. Another year that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You seem to be mentioning that people knew that deportation was bad, even if they didn't know what the-- the eventual destination was. What-- did you hear any rumors, or-- or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, why was this feared--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I guess is the-- see, this is what people-- actually, I get, sometimes, very mad. Because people don't understand it, that people did not know. There was denial on the people who were there. Absolute denial, to the last, [INAUDIBLE] unless we went to Auschwitz. Then we knew where we were. But there was denial. Because this is natural in a human, that if you give up and you think this is the end, then-- I mean, many people killed themself and-- and-- you know, committed suicide. We knew it's no good. But we didn't know where and what-- they always said that they-- they-- they'll relocate them. You know? Relocate them to-- in the work camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all we knew about it. Nobody could write from there. Even though, I mean, nobody could write for [INAUDIBLE]-- I-- I-- I don't know about anybody writing from there. Because they killed them, either they sent them into labor camps. But you couldn't sit down and write a letter back home, to [? home. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. At what point were you and your family finally deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in September, '42. My father was still schlepping the-- the-- the suitcases there. And that was actually the reason that we were kind of lucky, which you will find out that we went to Theresien, to Theresienstadt. Because we wouldn't have gone there. We would have gone to Poland. But because he worked there, this were the last big transport from Vienna, in September, 1942. Large transport. I don't know how many people. But maybe we are 500, maybe we're a thousand. But this was the last transport who went to Theresienstadt from-- from Vienna, was send away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, they were just small transport, were like a hundred people or 50 people. And then it stopped altogether, beginning in '93-- what is it? What year are we? '43. That ended then. But we-- we were send away in '42. What happened? Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this is when we got the invitation. And we knew already. We knew exactly where we have to appear. And we packed up our things. It was not that we had to do it hastily because we knew exactly where we going to go. And then we were collected there. And it was-- and-- and we-- we didn't-- really didn't know-- we had, I think we knew that we going to Theresienstadt. But it didn't mean anything to us. Because nobody wrote from there. I-- we-- nobody knew about it. And so for us, it was the same if they send us away from Theresienstadt someplace else. We didn't know where we are going. We were in-- in regular wagons, not in cattle wagons. And we were very tightly fitted into that. And-- and we send-- and that time, I-- I met already people are in the camp that-- that I knew, children that also went there. And then we arrived in-- in Theresien.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. But it-- it-- it was a day trip. It was maybe six, seven hours. Because it's not that far from-- from Austria. You know, it's not far from Prague. It's-- you know, it was not an-- I-- I remember when we arrived there it was daylight. So either we went through the night, or it was daylight when we arrived there. And then we had to get off the trains. And they took-- told us-- we packed a lot of things. But they told us we can only take what we can carry. Everything else we have to leave back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we grabbed whatever-- I-- I-- I remember-- see, I played accordion in-- in-- in Vienna. I took up accordion because with that, I figured I can take it every place. So I took my accordion along. It-- it was like-- it looked like a suitcase. So I grabbed that and something else, and left other things back. Which came pretty handy then, in the camp. Because I played for the children there, and they [LAUGHS] were dancing. So we had kind of made our own entertainment. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but it was awful when we started marching there, with the heavy suitcase. I remember my-- my father collapsed. He-- he-- he was like, it-- when he saw, the one who was so optimistic that we came in there, and the way it looked, he collapsed. But then, you know, we got him up. And we walked quite, quite a bit into-- into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, they had a lot of army barracks, you know? I don't know the-- in-- in-- in German, you say [GERMAN], which is a big-- it's not barracks. It's like big buildings. Because it was a garrison city, you know, where it's called Theresienstadt after Maria Theresa, you know? She was the-- the queen or the Empress of Austria. And the whole thing used to be all Austria-- Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before the First World War. So they brought us in this-- in this place, and they placed us in these barracks on the floor. And there we were and waited until whatever happened to us, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still together with your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We stayed for a while, you know? Then we found out there is a youth home in Theresien, for German and Austrian children. And we tried-- I think my mother tried to get me into that. Because it was the-- the life was impossible. Like, we just lived-- lived on the attic. There was attics and they're one next to the others, with their suitcases. And you couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got into this youth home, which I shared a room, maybe with 30, 35 children-- children. We-- I was 14. Some were 13. Some were 15. But they basically stayed together in-- in-- in certain ages. And my brother went to another youth home, also for-- for children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father later on was placed in another barrack. It's called the [NON-ENGLISH]. And my mother was in another one, where she lived with I don't know how many women, in three-story beds. And she had hers on the bottom, I remember. And I had mentioned my father was a pharmacist. So he got a job to run a pharmacy. They didn't have any medication. But the people who came, all this thousands of thousands of people, had medication in their suitcases, which they confiscated. And so they dispersed it in this-- in this pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I don't know, it wasn't much there. Maybe it was aspirin, it was-- whatever it was, he was able to-- to give it out for-- it was not major-- they didn't have penicillin that time, anyhow. So mainly, probably, aspirin and-- whatever. Whatever he had. So he was doing that. And my mother worked, too. They took her like a-- it's almost kind of a police, ghetto police, that they-- when people stood in line for-- for getting the food, they had to keep 'em so that-- you know, they had to take care of themself, that-- that-- Theresienstadt was a self-ruling-- I mean, the-- the-- the SS had-- was-- was the important people. But they wanted them to self-rule themselves. And there were leaders there. There were all kinds of-- of people who-- who took care, make sure that everything is running right, you know? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, Theresienstadt was a camp not where you went to stay. You came there to be sent away. This was-- this was-- most of the people came, were sent away. Like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So your mother and father were both working. How about your brother and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Me, I lived in that youth home. Most of the children went out and worked in the-- in the gardens. I mean, like, in-- like farmers, they were cutting the grass outside, and all kinds of things. I opted, like I-- I figured I-- at least when I'm there already, maybe I'll learn something. So I went into the-- where the seamstress, you know, where they-- what we did, we opened up old clothing. We completely disassembled and opened up all the seams. And then they showed us how to cut new ones out and then we sewed them again. So that's what I did. So I-- I-- I-- I stayed in the home and I sat and I was sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother worked-- I don't know. Either he was doing something in the-- it-- it's like in a-- with wood. He was carrying things. You know, they put him to work, whatever he did. Whatever was necessary, sometimes to carry the dead people, you know, they had to carry them out. Whatever was necessary to do, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your sleeping arrangements at Theresienstadt, in-- in the children's--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the children's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We were into-- we were in a room, I would say maybe it was 18 by 18, let's say, or 20 by 20 feet. And all around the wall, you had three story-- whatever you can-- bunks there. Bunk beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you have, if you're lucky-- and always two people sleeping together. You know? Two and two and two and two. And then-- it went-- So you'd have six.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- yeah. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stacked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six stacked. But it went all around the walls. And in the middle, we had-- we had a table. And we had washbasins, like, five or six washbasins. This was always the-- the funniest thing in the morning. It was not funny. Because whoever grew-- woke up first, they said, I'm first. So the first five could take a washbasin, took a little water. Wash yourself. You know, we had-- that's how we washed ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They always told me I was always the first one [LAUGHS] who called, I'm-- I am the first. Because I wanted to also get it over with. And then I had to go to work. And-- and we got to eat. We-- we got very, very little to eat. We got bread. We got a portion of bread. And then we got-- we had to stand in line for some kind of a soup. And once in a while, we got, like, something baked, something-- you know, that was a special thing. I remember-- and a piece of little margarine. And sometimes, a little sugar. And we all collected these things. And we made a cake out of it. We took the margarine and the sugar and we mixed it up. And we put it on the bread and another bread. And then we cut it and we pretended this was bread. And we liked it so much that they said, even if we ever come into freedom, this will be our favorite cake. [LAUGHTER] The-- the margarine sugar. But always hungry. Always hungry. That-- that was-- I know when I came to my parents, what did my father give us from-- you know, when you have diabetes, you get kind of a-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. What is it called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Insulin? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not insulin. It's called-- something you made out of grapes-- of-- of-- of-- of grapes. I forgot the name of it. But it's like a little [INAUDIBLE] and you-- you break it off. And you drink it. You can drink it. Because it-- it tastes sweet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- we broke that up and we drank that. He had this in his-- in his pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was-- it was an existence. You know, it was-- Theresienstadt was bad. But, it's coming worse later. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: We had-- I don't know, in 1943, I think it was when they had the-- or was it '44? When they had the assassination attempt on Hitler. They took us all into a big place. Everybody from Theresienstadt had to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, they set us up all in hundreds. Hundreds, hundreds, hundreds. And the-- the police were standing-- or the SS were standing here with guns. And at that time, the rumor went around that we are supposed to get killed there. They're just waiting for the-- form Berlin, that they have to let them know what to do. Couldn't go to the bathroom. It was-- it was awful. And it got dark. And all of a sudden, they said we can go. We can go back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you imagine? It was dark. And they were, like, maybe 60,000 people trying to get into the camp. It was pandemonium. It was terrible. But we made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think this was, either it was in '44-- I don't know, whenever the assassination-- somebody was assassinated. Maybe Heydrich was assassinated. There was a reason for them to do that. And-- and that's it. And life then went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then they started already people were taken. This one had to leave. And this one had to leave. And this one had to leave, until it was our turn, you know? Mm-hmm. You-- you mentioned you in a-- in a girls', you know, children's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorm, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --bunk, or dorm. Did you have any particular friends or people you were close with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I had-- I had, because what kept us really going is we were very Zionistic, very, you know, for-- for-- for Palestine, that time, there was no Israel. And our dream was, and I remember telling my parents, my parents-- my parents that-- that time they could still have children. I said, George and I will go to Palestine. So if we should get-- get out of here, we go to Palestine. So we-- I was in a group-- they called it the [NON-ENGLISH]-- with eight boys and four girls. And we met either they came over, or we went to their-- that was in my-- where my brother was, were the boys, and where we were, were the girls. So we had-- so-- a kind of a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did is basically, they taught us Israeli songs. And we were-- you know, we were kind of very enthusiastic about it. That kept us going. There's a certain-- and we even learned Hebrew from the people who knew. So there was one day that we said, we can only speak Hebrew to each other. I mean, we couldn't, but we-- we tried to. And I tell you, it helped me when I went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I could-- I could still read the signs and I could speak a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but that kind of-- you needed this, to-- to-- to just keep you going. And-- and because people kept leaving. People get-- kept dying. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My-- you know, my parents, they got skinnier and skinnier. It was-- it was kind of a-- and then-- we just existed there. And waited, whatever happened to us. You know? It was, like, two years that we spend here. What else did you do to try to keep yourself going and to-- to occupy your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, we had-- we were singing Hebrew songs. There were also, in our home, we put on-- up shows. You know, there were very talented kids there. And we put up shows and musicals, and so we sat together, and they were singing. And me, with my accordion, I was playing. And then I gave it to someone who played better than I, so they could also play the-- the songs. And we-- we [INAUDIBLE] that. They didn't know that we had-- that I had an accordion. They would have probably confiscated-- I mean, it stayed there anyhow. I mean, I lost it anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't take it to Auschwitz. And if I took it, I didn't get it. So, we just, you know, like, I worked. And then at-- at night, we-- there was nothing much to do. You know? So we-- we were always with girls together so we told each other stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Were you able to see your parents with any kind of regularity? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say regularly. But at least, once-- once a week. We-- we met in my father's little cubicle where he had the pharmacy. And we met there. And so we saw them. And once in a while, they came and visited me at the home there. But not too often. We couldn't get together too often.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll end tape two here and pick this up with tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
